card.title: How Vaccines Teach the Immune System
card.teaser: A vaccine is a flashcard for your immune system — a harmless preview of an enemy it might meet years from now.
card.coverImagePrompt: A syringe glowing with a radiant blue serum, tiny antibodies shaped like Y's orbiting the needle tip, medical macro cinematography, clean and hopeful

lessons.title: Milkmaids and cowpox
lessons.body: In 1796, a country doctor named Edward Jenner noticed something peculiar: milkmaids who had caught cowpox — a mild disease from cattle — seemed immune to smallpox, which killed one in three infected. He took pus from a milkmaid's cowpox sore and scratched it into a boy's arm. Weeks later he exposed the boy to smallpox. Nothing happened. That scratch, and the idea behind it, has since prevented more deaths than any single medical innovation in history.

visual.imagePrompt: An 18th-century pastoral scene of a milkmaid with a dairy cow, golden sunlight, while a doctor looks on thoughtfully

lessons.title: Your immune system keeps a photo album
lessons.body: When a new pathogen invades, specialized cells called B cells make antibodies — Y-shaped proteins that latch onto the invader and mark it for destruction. It takes a week or two to mobilize. But afterward, memory cells save a 'photo' of the enemy's shape. If the same pathogen shows up again, the response is faster and fiercer — often before you even feel sick.

keyTerms.definition: A protein that recognizes and binds to a specific pathogen
An immune cell that remembers past invaders for decades

visual.imagePrompt: A stack of labeled photo cards showing silhouettes of viruses and bacteria, each filed under a glowing B cell

lessons.title: Teaching without the danger
lessons.body: Vaccines hand your immune system the 'photo' without the fight. Some use a weakened version of the pathogen (MMR). Some use a killed version (flu shot). Some use just a protein subunit (HPV). The newer mRNA vaccines take yet another path: they deliver genetic instructions that make your own cells temporarily produce a tiny, harmless piece of the virus — just enough to train the immune system. No live virus is ever introduced.

keyTerms.definition: A weakened live pathogen, unable to cause disease but still recognizable
A vaccine using genetic instructions to make your cells produce a target protein

visual.imagePrompt: A tiny lecture hall where immune cells are students, a chalkboard showing a drawing of a virus, no danger in sight

lessons.title: A wiped-out disease
lessons.body: Smallpox killed an estimated 300 million people in the 20th century alone. By 1980, after a global vaccination campaign, the World Health Organization declared it eradicated — the only human disease ever eliminated. Polio paralyzed hundreds of thousands of children each year in the 1950s; today, fewer than a hundred wild cases occur annually. Measles deaths have dropped by over 80% since 2000 thanks to vaccines.
lessons.funFact: The last natural smallpox case was diagnosed in Somalia in 1977. The patient, Ali Maow Maalin, survived — and later spent decades helping eradicate polio in Africa.

visual.imagePrompt: A dramatic graph of smallpox deaths crashing to zero across the 20th century, labeled with milestones

lessons.title: Herd immunity is a team sport
lessons.body: Vaccines protect you — but they also protect people who can't be vaccinated: newborns, cancer patients, the elderly. When enough people are immune, the pathogen can't find enough hosts to keep spreading. That's herd immunity, and it's why a dip in vaccination rates can bring diseases roaring back. Your immune system learning a lesson is everyone's problem, in the best possible way.

quiz.question: What do vaccines give your immune system?
options: A safe preview of a pathogen so it can prepare memory cells
quiz.explanation: Vaccines expose the immune system to a harmless version or fragment of the pathogen, building memory.

quiz.question: How does an mRNA vaccine differ from older vaccines?
options: It contains the whole killed virus
It delivers instructions so your own cells briefly make a harmless piece of the pathogen
It is applied to the skin
quiz.explanation: mRNA vaccines carry genetic instructions — your cells produce a target protein, triggering immunity.

quiz.question: What is herd immunity?
options: When enough of a population is immune that the pathogen can't spread effectively
A type of natural immunity only
quiz.explanation: High immunity in a group starves pathogens of new hosts, protecting vulnerable people indirectly.

sources.title: Smallpox Eradication: Destruction of Variola Virus Stocks
